analogy: Think of "Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion" through the chapter's own opening images: The family, caste, tribe and the market – these are the social institutions that have been considered in the last two chapters. These institutions were seen from the point of view of their role in forming communities and sustaining society. In this chapter we consider... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 5.1 What Is S ocial about socIal, 5.2 Caste and tribe – systems Justifying and, THe POVeRTY lINe, 2011–12, RURAL INDIA URBAN INDIA. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that the family, caste, tribe and the market – these are the social institutions that have been considered in the last two chapters.

The text explains that these institutions were seen from the point of view of their role in forming communities and sustaining society.

The text explains that in this chapter we consider an equally important aspect of such institutions, namely their role in creating and sustaining patterns of inequality and exclusion.

The text explains that for most of us who are born and live in India, social inequality and exclusion are facts of life.

The text explains that we see beggars in the streets and on railway platforms.

The text explains that we see young children labouring as domestic workers, construction helpers, cleaners and helpers in streetside restaurants (dhabas) and tea-shops.

The text explains that we are not surprised at the sight of small children, who work as domestic workers in middle class urban homes, carrying the school bags of older children to school.

The text explains that it does not immediately strike us as unjust that some children are denied schooling.

The text explains that some of us read about caste discrimination against children in schools; some of us face it.

The text explains that likewise, news reports about violence against women and prejudice against minority groups and the differently abled are part of our everyday lives.

The text explains that this everydayness of social inequality and exclusion often make them appear inevitable, almost natural.

The text explains that if we do sometimes recognise that inequality and exclusion are not inevitable, we often think of them as being ‘deserved’ or ‘justified’ in some sense.

The text explains that perhaps the poor and marginalised are where they are because they are lacking in ability, or haven’t tried hard enough to improve their situation?

The text explains that we thus tend to blame them for their own plight – if only they worked harder or were more intelligent, they wouldn’t be where they are.

The text explains that a closer examination will show that few work harder than those who are located at the lower ranks of society.

The text explains that as a South American proverb says – “If hard labour were really such a good thing, the rich would keep it all for themselves!” All over the world, back-breaking work like stone breaking, digging, carrying heavy weights, pulling rickshaws or carts is invariably done by the poor.

The text explains that it is these non-individual or group differences that explain social inequality and exclusion.

The text explains that the question being asked in this section has three broad answers which may be stated briefly as follows.

The text explains that third, they are systematic and structured – there is a definite pattern to social inqualities.

The text explains that s ociAl InequAlity In every society, some people have a greater share of valued resources – money, property, education, health, and power – than others.

The text explains that these social resources can be divided into three forms of capital – economic capital in the form of material assets and income; cultural capital such as educational qualifications and status; and social capital in the form of networks of contacts and social associations (Bourdieu 1986).

The text explains that patterns of unequal access to social resources are commonly called social inequality.

The text explains that however, by and large, social inequality is not the outcome of innate or ‘natural’ differences between people, but is produced by the society in which they live.

The text explains that sociologists use the term social stratification to refer to a system by which categories of people in a society are ranked in a hierarchy.

The text explains that this hierarchy then shapes people’s identity and experiences, their relations with others, as well as their access to resources and opportunities.

The text explains that three key principles help explain social stratification: 1.

The text explains that social stratification is a characteristic of society, not simply a function of individual differences.

The text explains that social stratification is a society-wide system that unequally distributes social resources among categories of people.

The text explains that in more technologically advanced societies where people produce a surplus over and above their basic needs, however, social resources are unequally distributed to various social categories regardless of people’s innate individual abilities.

The text explains that it is closely linked to the family and to the inheritance of social resources from one generation to the next.

The text explains that within the caste system, birth dictates occupational opportunities.

The text explains that no system of social stratification is likely to persist over generations unless it is widely viewed as being either fair or inevitable.

The text explains that the caste system, for example, is justified in terms of the opposition of purity and pollution, with the Brahmins designated as the most superior and Dalits as the most inferior by virtue of their birth and occupation.

The text explains that not everyone, though, thinks of a system of inequality as legitimate.

The text explains that typically, people with the greatest social privileges express the strongest support for systems of stratification such as caste and race.

The text explains that often we discuss social exclusion and discrimination as though they pertain to differential economic resources alone.

The text explains that a prejudiced person’s preconceived views are often based on hearsay rather than on direct evidence, and are resistant to change even in the face of new information.

The text explains that discriminatory behaviour or practices may be presented as motivated by other, more justifiable, reasons rather than prejudice.

The text explains that s ociAl ExcluSion Social exclusion refers to ways in which individuals may become cut off from full involvement in the wider society.

The text explains that social exclusion is not accidental but systematic – it is the result of structural features of society.

The text explains that at different periods of history protest movements arose against caste, gender and religious discrimination.

The text explains that thus, legislation alone is unable to transform society or produce lasting social change.

The text explains that you have already read about the impact of colonialism on Indian society.

The text explains that in this chapter we focus on four such groups who have suffered from serious social inequality and exclusion, namely Dalits or the ex-untouchable castes; adivasis or communities refered to as ‘tribal’; women, and the differently abled.

The text explains that 5.2 Caste and tribe – systems Justifying and Perpetuating Inequality The CasTe SysTem as a DiscriminaTory SysTem The caste system is a distinct Indian social institution that legitimises and enforces practices of discrimination against people born into particular castes.

The text explains that historically, the caste system classified people by their occupation and status.

The text explains that (Compare this to the ‘apartheid’ system described in Box 5.1b) However, in actual historical practice economic and social status tended to coincide.

The text explains that there was thus a fairly close correlation between social (i.e. caste) status and economic status – the ‘high’ castes were almost invariably of high economic status, while the ‘low’ castes were almost always of low economic Trans Gender – In general ‘male body’ and ‘female body’ as social unit are unchanging identity but due to so many researches in the field of physiology, the notion of the body is now linked with ‘choice structure’.

The text explains that 2002. ‘Marginalisation and Disability: experiences from the third world’, in Priestly, M. ed.

The text explains that disability and the Life Course: Global Perspectives.

The text explains that jeffery, Craig, Jeffery, Roger. and Jeffery, Patricia.

The text explains that 2005. ‘Broken Trajectories: Dalit Young Men and Formal Education’, in Chopra, Radhika. and Jeffery, Patricia. ed.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Indian Society, Chapter 5: Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion

mcqs.question_text: In the NCERT discussion of Patterns of Social Inequality and Exclusion, which statement is supported by the source text about GROUPS?
options: First, social inequality and exclusion are social because they are not about individuals but about groups.
Second, they are social in the sense that they are not economic, although there is usually a strong link between social and economic inequality.
Third, they are systematic and structured – there is a definite pattern to social inqualities.
These three broad senses of the ‘social’ will be explored briefly below.
